content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      : File                                          Date  : Jan. 18, 1977


From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION              A 77-01-237
         Ted Prim

Subject: Definition of a Committee.

         Philip Martin, an attorney in San Francisco, asked whether
    a legal defense fund for Sheriff Hongisto constitutes a com-
    mittee within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. I
    advised him on January 13, 1977, that the legal defense fund
    would be a recipient committee pursuant to Section 82013(a).

         2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215 (b)(1) provides that any payment to
    a candidate is a contribution unless it is clear from all the
    surrounding circumstances that the payment was for personal
    purposes unrelated to the person's candidacy or status as an
    officeholder. Initially, we observe that the legal defense
    fund is related to Sheriff Hongisto's status as an office-
    holder since the legal difficulties involved emanate directly
    from his refusal to perform certain duties in his capacity
    as sheriff. Furthermore, we think that Sheriff Hongisto is
    a candidate at the present time. Section 82007 provides that
    a person is a candidate whenever he raises or expends funds,
    or authorizes another to do so on his behalf, for the purpose
    of seeking nomination or election to elective office regard-
    less of whether the office is known at the present time. In
    the instant case, Sheriff Hongisto has held at least two fund-
    raising dinners since his last election to reduce previous
    campaign debts. Because it is generally advisable to liqui-
    date past campaign debts before seeking future office, the
    staff assumes that such fund-raising activities are generally
    undertaken with some view of seeking future office, albeit
    sometimes remote.

         2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215 provides that a candidate re-
    ceives a contribution when he receives payment or when a pay-
    ment is made at his behest. Section [illegible] that regulation
    provides that payment is made at the behest of the candidate
    when it is made pursuant to his control or direction. In the
    instant case, we think payments by the legal defense fund are
    made at the behest of Sheriff Hongisto since such funds will
    be used to pay the cost of transcripts, fines, and attorney's
    fees. Because Sheriff Hongisto is the defendant, we think that

                                                                    [1]

File
Jan. 18, 1977
p.2


he necessarily exercises control and direction over the defense
case.

Subsection (d) of the regulation provides that a contribution
includes any monetary or non-monetary payment for which full
and adequate consideration is not made to the donor that is
received by any person or organization other than candidate,
control committee, official arm of a political party, or
primary purpose committee if the payment is earmarked for the
making of contributions or expenditures. A payment is ear-
marked when at the time of making the payment the donor knows
or has reason to know that the payment or funds with which the
payment will be commingled will be used to make contributions.
In the instant case, the donors know that the funds will be
used to assist Sheriff Hongisto in his legal defense. Since
these payments are under the control and direction of candi-
date Hongisto in connection with his status as an officeholder,
such payments are earmarked as contributions Since the payments
are contributions, the legal defense fund will be a committee
pursuant to Section 82013(a) if it receives $500 or more in
donations. In addition, a person could become a major donor
pursuant to Section 82013(c) by virtue of such payments to the
legal defense fund.


TP:jo

See also A-99-02-035

sections.question: Does a legal defense fund for Sheriff Hongisto constitute a committee within the meaning of the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion: The legal defense fund constitutes a recipient committee pursuant to Section 82013(a) because payments to it are contributions under the Political Reform Act. The legal defense fund will be a committee if it receives $500 or more in donations, and donors could become major donors pursuant to Section 82013(c).
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a legal defense fund established for Sheriff Hongisto constitutes a committee under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the legal defense fund is a recipient committee under Section 82013(a) because payments to it qualify as contributions, as Sheriff Hongisto is a candidate who exercises control over the fund's use for his legal defense related to his officeholder status.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal FPPC memorandum dated January 18, 1977, documenting oral advice given by Ted Prim to attorney Philip Martin on January 13, 1977. No formal question/conclusion sections present, but question and conclusion are clearly identifiable from the narrative text. Document provides detailed regulatory analysis and reasoning.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does a legal defense fund for Sheriff Hongisto constitute a committee within the meaning of the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: The legal defense fund constitutes a recipient committee pursuant to Section 82013(a) because payments to it are contributions under the Political Reform Act. The legal defense fund will be a committee if it receives $500 or more in donations, and donors could become major donors pursuant to Section 82013(c).

embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum addressing whether a legal defense fund for Sheriff Hongisto constitutes a committee under the Political Reform Act. The Commission concluded it does constitute a recipient committee because donations are contributions under the Act, as Hongisto is a candidate and the funds are under his control for defense costs related to his officeholder status.
